question_title: Will the interest in “harry dresden” change between 2026-04-13 and 2026-04-21 according to Google Trends?
summary: The search term "harry dresden" experienced a massive viral spike (0→100) around April 10-11, 2026, driven by a viral Ring camera video of Jason Thomas Nichols breaking into a home while identifying himself as "Harry Dresden." The term's baseline is 0-1. The current value on Apr 13 is 46 (in the resolution date range), already decaying rapidly from the peak. Even under the most conservative decay scenario (5%/day), the projected Apr 21 value (~30) is far below the 43 threshold needed for "Doesn't change." The Monte Carlo simulation produced 100% Decreases across 100k trials. Major YouTube creators already amplified the story on Apr 11-12, meaning the secondary wave has passed. Reserving small probability for unexpected viral resurgence (court appearance, new developments) and measurement uncertainty.

factors.description: Viral meme spike is decaying rapidly: peak 100 on Apr 11 → 53 → 46. Even at slowest plausible decay (5%/day), value reaches ~30 by Apr 21, well below the 43 threshold needed for 'Doesn't change'.
Baseline interest in 'harry dresden' is 0-1 (essentially zero). The spike is entirely from a one-off viral meme, not sustained organic interest. No structural reason for interest to remain elevated.

Major YouTube creators (penguinz0) already covered the meme on Apr 11-12, meaning the secondary amplification wave has already occurred. Typical meme lifecycle: original post → creator coverage → rapid decay.
The arrested individual (Jason Thomas Nichols) could generate follow-up news from court appearances or new developments, potentially causing a secondary interest spike.
factors.conditional: If court appearance or major new development goes viral

factors.description: Resolution uses SerpAPI with tz=0, which may differ slightly from pytrends values. At these levels (46), a ±2 measurement difference doesn't change the outcome since the expected decline is 15-30+ points.

reasoning:
**Forecast: Decreases (93%)**

Key reasoning:
- "Harry dresden" has a baseline of 0-1 in Google Trends (US). A viral Ring camera meme caused a spike to 100 on Apr 11, now decaying (53 → 46 on Apr 12-13).
- The resolution compares Apr 21 to Apr 13 (value: 46) with a ±3 threshold. "Decreases" requires Apr 21 < 43.
- Even the most conservative decay model (5%/day) projects ~30 by Apr 21 — well below 43.
- The YouTube amplification wave (penguinz0, etc.) already occurred on Apr 11-12, so the story has passed peak virality.
- Small residual probability (7%) allocated to "Doesn't change" (5%) and "Increases" (2%) for unforeseen viral events or measurement surprises.
resolution_criteria: This question resolves “Doesn't change” if the value on the timeline at [https://trends.google.com/trends/explore?geo=US&tz=0&q=harry%20dresden&date=2026-03-22%202026-04-21](https://trends.google.com/trends/explore?geo=US&tz=0&q=harry%20dresden&date=2026-03-22%202026-04-21) for 2026-04-21 is within 3 of the value at 2026-04-13. It resolves “Increases” if the value is more than 3 greater, and resolves “Decreases” if the value is more than 3 lower.
fine_print:
A script will be used to determine the resolution of this question. It will access the data on Google Trends using [SerpApi](https://serpapi.com/), and compare the height of the timeline for the two aforementioned dates. The specific python query will be structured as follows:
```
from serpapi import GoogleSearch

search = GoogleSearch(params={
  "api_key": API_KEY,
  "engine": "google_trends",
  "data_type": "TIMESERIES",
  "geo": "US",
  "tz": 0,
  "q": "harry dresden",
  "date": "2026-03-22 2026-04-21",
})
search.get_dict()
```
Note that there may be differences between the results returned by the API and the data appearing on the page. This seems to be due to the 'tz' parameter not having the intended effect in-browser. In this case, the API results will be considered authoritative.
Note that the precision of the timeline will be 1 day, so this will compare the overall interest for the whole day as determined by Google Trends.
Dates are determined in UTC.
If the data is no longer available, or the script fails, this question will be annulled or manually resolved by a moderator.
